section.title: God of Vengeance, Shine Forth
section.sectionRationale: A communal lament crying out to the God of vengeance against the arrogant who oppress the vulnerable and mock God's sight, this psalm moves from urgent protest to confident trust, resting in the LORD as stronghold and rock of refuge, the very justice ultimately entrusted to Christ, the appointed Judge.
section.thesis: The psalmist cries to the God of vengeance to rise up as Judge against the wicked who crush the weak and mock God's awareness, and finds settled confidence that the LORD, who disciplines and never forsakes His own, will turn the wickedness of the wicked back upon themselves.
section.frameNote: Six movements: an urgent cry to the Judge of the earth, verses 1-3; a portrait of the arrogant wicked, verses 4-7; a rebuke of the foolish who doubt God sees, verses 8-11; the blessing of divine discipline and certain justice, verses 12-15; a personal testimony of God's help, verses 16-19; and confidence in God as the final Judge, verses 20-23.

units.label: Rise Up, O Judge of the Earth

verses.text: O LORD, God of {{naqam|vengeance}}, O God of vengeance, shine forth.
Rise up, O Judge of the earth; render a reward to the proud.
How long will the wicked, O LORD, how long will the wicked exult?

units.label: They Crush Your People
units.note: a portrait of the arrogant wicked

verses.text: They pour out arrogant words; all workers of iniquity boast.
They crush Your people, O LORD; they oppress Your heritage.
They kill the widow and the foreigner; they murder the fatherless.
They say, "The LORD does not see; the God of Jacob pays no heed."

units.label: He Who Formed the Eye, Can He Not See

verses.text: Take notice, O senseless among the people! O fools, when will you be wise?
He who affixed the ear, can He not hear? He who formed the eye, can He not see?
He who admonishes the nations, does He not discipline? He who teaches man, does He lack knowledge?
The LORD knows the thoughts of man, that they are futile.

units.label: Blessed Is the Man You Discipline
units.note: the blessing of discipline and certain justice

verses.text: Blessed is the man You {{yasar|discipline}}, O LORD, and teach from Your law,
to grant him relief from days of trouble, until a pit is dug for the wicked.
For the LORD will not forsake His people; He will never abandon His heritage.
Surely {{mishpat|judgment}} will again be righteous, and all the upright in heart will follow it.

units.label: Your Loving Devotion Supports Me
units.note: a personal testimony of God's help

verses.text: Who will rise up for me against the wicked? Who will stand for me against the workers of iniquity?
Unless the LORD had been my helper, I would soon have dwelt in the abode of silence.
If I say, "My foot is slipping," Your loving devotion, O LORD, supports me.
When anxiety overwhelms me, Your consolation delights my soul.

units.label: The Lord Is My Rock of Refuge

verses.text: Can a corrupt throne be Your ally, one devising mischief by decree?
They band together against the righteous and condemn the innocent to death.
But the LORD has been my {{misgab|stronghold}}, and my God is my rock of refuge.
He will bring upon them their own iniquity and destroy them for their wickedness. The LORD our God will destroy them.

naqam.gloss: Vengeance, just retribution for wrong done. The psalmist appeals to this quality of God not as personal revenge but as the righteous judgment only the sovereign Judge of the earth may rightly execute.

yasar.gloss: To discipline, correct, or instruct. God's discipline of His own people is presented here as a blessing, distinct from His judgment on the unrepentant wicked, since it is aimed at teaching and relief, not destruction.

mishpat.gloss: Judgment, justice, the exercise of righteous legal decision. The psalmist trusts that this quality of God's rule will again prevail, however delayed it may currently seem.

misgab.gloss: A stronghold, a high, secure place of defense. The psalm closes by naming the LORD Himself this stronghold, the settled ground of confidence even while corrupt human power seems to prevail.

basic.context: The psalmist cries out to the God of vengeance against arrogant oppressors who crush the vulnerable, the widow, the foreigner, and the fatherless, and who mock God's very awareness of their crimes. The psalm moves from urgent protest to settled trust that the LORD disciplines but never abandons His people, and will destroy the wicked in the end.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes a specific community's experience of injustice under corrupt power, and it prescribes an abiding pattern: bringing outrage over injustice honestly to God as the rightful Judge, rather than taking vengeance into human hands.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist appeals to the LORD as the God of naqam, vengeance, verse 1, distinguishing God's righteous yasar, discipline, of His own people, verse 12, from His judgment on the wicked. He trusts that mishpat, justice, verse 15, will prevail, and closes calling God his misgab, stronghold, verse 22.
basic.typology: The psalm's cry for God to rise up as Judge of the earth, and its confidence that He will not forsake His heritage, anticipates the New Testament's teaching that God has appointed a day when He will judge the world in righteousness through Christ.
basic.christ: God the Father judges no one but has given all judgment to the Son, John 5:22, so the vindication this psalm cries out for is entrusted fully to Jesus Christ. At the cross, apparent injustice, the condemnation of the truly innocent, was itself the means by which God's ultimate justice and mercy were accomplished, Acts 2:23-24.
basic.god: God is shown as the rightful avenger of injustice, the Judge of the earth who sees and hears everything, who lovingly disciplines His own people for their good, and who remains their stronghold and rock of refuge even while corrupt power seems, for a time, to prevail.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 12:19 teaches believers to leave vengeance to God rather than avenge themselves. Hebrews 12:6 affirms that the Lord disciplines the one He loves. John 5:22 shows the Father has entrusted all judgment to the Son.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's cry for the God of vengeance to shine forth and judge the wicked, and its confidence that God will never abandon His people, is answered fully in Christ, to whom the Father has given all judgment, and who at the cross turned apparent injustice into the very means of the world's redemption.

context.literary: A communal lament in six movements, moving from urgent protest against the wicked, verses 1-7, through a wisdom rebuke of the foolish, verses 8-11, to a beatitude on divine discipline, verses 12-15, a personal testimony, verses 16-19, and closing confidence in God as stronghold and Judge, verses 20-23.
context.historical: Likely composed during a season of corrupt leadership, when those in power used legal decrees to oppress the vulnerable, widows, foreigners, and orphans, matching the prophetic critiques of social injustice found in Isaiah, Amos, and Micah.
context.canonical: Standing within the cluster of psalms in Book IV that celebrate the LORD's kingship after the crisis of Psalm 89, this psalm shows that the LORD's reign includes His role as the Judge who will not allow injustice to stand forever.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice honest outrage over entrenched, systemic injustice to the LORD as the rightful Judge of the earth, and to move the community from protest to settled trust in God's certain, though sometimes delayed, justice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific community's suffering under corrupt, oppressive power; it prescribes bringing such outrage directly and honestly to God rather than either despairing of justice or seizing vengeance for oneself.

debates.question: Does verse 20's 'corrupt throne' refer to a foreign oppressive power or corrupt leadership within Israel itself?

views.label: It refers to corrupt leadership within Israel, using the law itself to oppress the vulnerable
views.case: Verse 20's language of a throne 'devising mischief by decree' points to abuse of legitimate legal authority, matching the prophets' repeated critiques of Israel's own corrupt judges and rulers who exploited widows, foreigners, and orphans under cover of law.

views.label: It refers to a foreign occupying power ruling unjustly over God's people
views.case: The psalm's earlier language of enemies crushing 'Your heritage,' verse 5, may point instead to a foreign ruling power oppressing Israel from outside, a common experience in the exilic and post-exilic periods.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm insists that no human throne, whether within Israel or over her, escapes accountability to the LORD, the true Judge of the earth, who will not let injustice committed under any legal cover stand forever.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist calls on the LORD as the God of naqam, vengeance, verse 1, distinguishing this righteous judgment from His fatherly yasar, discipline, of His own people, verse 12. He trusts that mishpat, justice, verse 15, will again prevail, and finds his settled confidence in the LORD as his misgab, stronghold, verse 22, even while a corrupt throne devises mischief by decree.

typology.type: The cry for God to rise up as Judge of the earth and render just reward, verses 1-2
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the appointment of a day when God will judge the world in righteousness through Jesus Christ, whom He has given all authority to judge, John 5:22, Acts 17:31.

typology.type: The condemnation of the innocent to death by a corrupt throne, verses 20-21
typology.fulfillment: Answered supremely at the cross, where the truly innocent One was condemned to death, yet God turned this apparent injustice into the world's redemption, Acts 2:23-24.

study.christ: This psalm's cry for the God of vengeance to rise up and judge righteously is answered in the truth that the Father has given all judgment to the Son, John 5:22. And its lament that a corrupt throne condemns the innocent to death, verse 21, is most fully realized and then reversed at the cross, where Jesus, wholly innocent, was condemned by corrupt human authority, yet God raised Him up, turning apparent defeat into the decisive victory over sin and injustice, Acts 2:23-24.
study.god: God is the rightful Judge and avenger of wrong, fully aware of every deed since He formed both eye and ear, verse 9. He lovingly disciplines His own people for their good, verse 12, remains their stronghold even under corrupt human power, and will certainly bring the wickedness of the wicked back upon their own heads.

crossReferences.note: Believers are called to leave vengeance to God rather than avenge themselves, trusting Him as this psalm's rightful Judge.
The Lord disciplines the one He loves, echoing this psalm's beatitude on divine correction, verse 12.
The Father has entrusted all judgment to the Son, fulfilling this psalm's cry for the Judge of the earth to rise up.

study.oneStory: This psalm's honest cry against entrenched injustice and its settled trust that God will not forsake His people find their fullest answer in Christ, to whom all judgment has been entrusted, and who at the cross turned the world's greatest apparent injustice into its redemption.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God to do in verses 1-2?

options.text: Shine forth as the God of vengeance and rise up as Judge of the earth to reward the proud.
options.note: These opening verses are an urgent plea for God to act as the rightful Judge against injustice.

options.text: Remain silent and inactive forever.
options.note: The plea is the opposite, urging God to act decisively, not remain inactive.

options.text: Give the psalmist personal authority to punish his enemies.
options.note: The psalmist appeals to God to act, not to be given personal authority to take vengeance himself.

options.text: Judge the earth with perfect righteousness.
options.note: This is true and closely related, though verses 1-2 specifically call on God as the God of vengeance to shine forth and reward the proud, a more pointed plea than a general statement about judgment.

quiz.q: According to verses 5-7, whom do the wicked oppress, and what do they claim about God?

options.text: They crush the widow, the foreigner, and the fatherless, claiming the LORD does not see.
options.note: These verses name the specific vulnerable groups oppressed and the wicked's false claim of divine blindness.

options.text: They protect the vulnerable and honor God's sight.
options.note: The text describes the opposite: oppression of the vulnerable and denial of God's awareness.

options.text: They oppress only foreign armies, not ordinary people.
options.note: The specific victims named are the widow, foreigner, and fatherless, common people, not armies.

options.text: They boast in arrogant words and consider themselves unaccountable.
options.note: This is true and closely related, verse 4, though the specific claim named in verse 7 is that the LORD does not see or pay heed.

quiz.q: What rebuke does the psalmist give the foolish in verses 8-9?

options.text: That the God who formed the eye and ear surely sees and hears everything.
options.note: These verses argue from God's role as Creator to His full awareness of human deeds.

options.text: That God has no ability to perceive human actions.
options.note: The rebuke argues the opposite: God's Creator status guarantees His full awareness.

options.text: That wisdom is unattainable for anyone.
options.note: The passage calls fools to become wise, implying wisdom is attainable, not impossible.

options.text: That the wicked are foolish for doubting God's awareness.
options.note: This is essentially correct in substance, though the specific argument given in verse 9 reasons from God's role as Creator of the eye and ear to His certain awareness.

options.text: The one whom the LORD disciplines and teaches from His law.
options.note: This verse presents God's loving discipline and instruction as a genuine blessing.

options.text: The one who avoids all correction from God.
options.note: The blessing described is receiving God's discipline and instruction, not avoiding it.

options.text: The one who never experiences any trouble.
options.note: Verse 13 describes relief from days of trouble, implying trouble is present, not absent.

options.text: The one who receives relief during days of trouble.
options.note: This is true and part of the same thought, verse 13, though verse 12 specifically names the blessing as being disciplined and taught from God's law.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's cry for justice find its ultimate answer according to the New Testament?

options.text: God has entrusted all judgment to Jesus Christ the Son.
options.note: John 5:22 shows the Father has given all judgment to the Son, fulfilling this psalm's cry for the rightful Judge to rise up.

options.text: It has no connection to any New Testament teaching.
options.note: The New Testament directly addresses divine judgment and vengeance in relation to Christ.

options.text: It is answered only through human courts and legal systems.
options.note: The psalm looks to God Himself, ultimately fulfilled in Christ, as the true Judge, not merely human legal systems.

options.text: It calls believers to leave vengeance to God rather than avenge themselves.
options.note: This is true, Romans 12:19, and closely related, though the fullest New Testament answer is that the Father has given all judgment specifically to the Son, John 5:22.

questions: The psalmist cries out honestly against injustice rather than staying silent. Is there an injustice in your world right now that you need to bring honestly before God in prayer?
The wicked claim, 'The LORD does not see.' Where might you be tempted to act as though God is not truly aware of what is happening in your life or in the world?
Verse 12 calls God's discipline a blessing. How do you typically respond to correction, whether from God or others, and can you see a difficult season of discipline as ultimately for your good?
The psalmist testifies, 'Your loving devotion, O LORD, supports me,' when his foot was slipping. What is currently causing you to feel unsteady, and how might you lean more fully on God's support?
This psalm's cry for justice is entrusted to Christ, to whom all judgment has been given. How does trusting Jesus as the appointed Judge free you from needing to secure justice on your own?

translationNotes.note: God of vengeance renders a Hebrew phrase describing rightful judicial retribution belonging to God alone, not personal or vindictive revenge.
This verse is directly quoted in 1 Corinthians 3:20, where Paul applies it to the futility of merely human wisdom before God.
Condemn the innocent to death can also be rendered condemn innocent blood, emphasizing the severity of judicial injustice the psalm protests.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 94:1-23, 23 verses). No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed this session as a dedicated message on Psalm 94, so status is none.
